name.text: Week 11 – Q5: Self-Inductance of a Solenoid & Inductor Energy

questiontext.text:
Self-Inductance of a Solenoid & Inductor Energy

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 4 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 0%

A solenoid has \(N = {@N_val@}\) turns, radius \(r = {@r_cm@}\;\text{cm}\), and length \(\ell = {@ell_cm@}\;\text{cm}\). It carries a DC current of \(I = {@I_val@}\;\text{A}\).

Assume the magnetic field inside the solenoid is uniform and the permeability is \(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\;\text{H/m}\).

(a) [1 pt] Compute the self-inductance \(L\) of the solenoid (in mH).

[[input:ans1]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number in mH, e.g. 0.395 or 3.95e-1

[[validation:ans1]]

(b) [1 pt] Compute the energy stored in the inductor (in mJ).

[[input:ans2]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number in mJ, e.g. 0.296 or 2.96e-1

[[validation:ans2]]

(c) [1 pt] A student computed \(L = {@float(L_wrong_no_N2*1000)@}\;\text{mH}\) for this solenoid. What error did they make?

[[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]]

(d) [1 pt] If the current through the solenoid is doubled (from \(I\) to \(2I\)), by what factor does the stored energy change?

[[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

(a) Self-Inductance

The self-inductance of a solenoid is given by:

\[L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{\ell}\]

where \(A = \pi r^2\) is the cross-sectional area.

First, convert to SI units:

  • \(r = {@r_cm@}\;\text{cm} = {@r_val@}\;\text{m}\)
  • \(\ell = {@ell_cm@}\;\text{cm} = {@ell_val@}\;\text{m}\)

Then:

\[A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times ({@r_val@})^2 = {@A_val@}\;\text{m}^2\]

\[L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times {@N_val@}^2 \times {@A_val@}}{@ell_val@} = {@L_val@}\;\text{H} = {@L_mH@}\;\text{mH}\]

(b) Energy Stored

The energy stored in an inductor is:

\[W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times {@L_val@} \times {@I_val@}^2 = {@W_val@}\;\text{J} = {@W_mJ@}\;\text{mJ}\]

(c) Debugging

The student obtained \(L = {@float(L_wrong_no_N2*1000)@}\;\text{mH}\). Comparing with the correct value \(L = {@L_mH@}\;\text{mH}\), the ratio is \({@L_mH@} / {@float(L_wrong_no_N2*1000)@} = {@N_val@}\). This means the student used \(L = \mu_0 N A / \ell\) instead of \(L = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell\) — they forgot one factor of \(N\).

(d) Energy Scaling

Since \(W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2\) and \(L\) is independent of the current:

\[W' = \tfrac{1}{2} L (2I)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} L \cdot 4I^2 = 4 \times \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2 = 4W\]

The energy quadruples when the current is doubled.

Answer Summary

PartQuantityCorrect Answer
(a)Self-inductance \(L\){@ta1@} mH
(b)Stored energy \(W\){@ta2@} mJ
(c)Error identificationUsed \(N\) instead of \(N^2\)
(d)Energy scaling factorEnergy quadruples (\(W \propto I^2\))

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — Self-Inductance

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Stored Energy

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — Identify the Error

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — Energy Scaling

[[feedback:prt4]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: N={@N_val@}, r={@r_cm@}cm, ℓ={@ell_cm@}cm, I={@I_val@}A | L={@ta1@} mH, W={@ta2@} mJ

questiondescription.text: Self-inductance of a solenoid and inductor energy storage — compute L = μ₀N²A/ℓ and W = ½LI², debug a common error, and analyze energy scaling. Week 11 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: Close! Your answer is within 15% of the expected value. Check your arithmetic or rounding. The exact answer is {@ta1@} mH.

node.description: Check if student used N instead of N²

truefeedback.text: It looks like you used \(L = \mu_0 N A / \ell\) instead of \(L = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell\). The inductance depends on \(N^2\), not \(N\).

node.description: Check if student swapped area and length

truefeedback.text: It looks like you swapped the cross-sectional area and length. The correct formula is \(L = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell\), with the area in the numerator and the length in the denominator.
Your answer is off by about a factor of 10. Check your unit conversions (cm to m) or decimal placement.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The self-inductance is \(L = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell = {@ta1@}\;\text{mH}\). Make sure to convert the radius and length from cm to m, use \(A = \pi r^2\), and include \(N^2\) (not \(N\)).

truefeedback.text: Close! Your answer is within 15% of the expected value. Check your arithmetic or rounding. The exact answer is {@ta2@} mJ.
Your answer is off by about a factor of 10. Check your unit conversions or whether you converted L and W to mH and mJ correctly.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The stored energy is \(W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2 = {@ta2@}\;\text{mJ}\). Use \(L = {@L_val@}\;\text{H}\) and \(I = {@I_val@}\;\text{A}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! The student used \(L = \mu_0 N A / \ell\) instead of \(L = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell\), missing one factor of \(N\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Compare the student's value \({@float(L_wrong_no_N2*1000)@}\;\text{mH}\) with the correct value \({@ta1@}\;\text{mH}\). The ratio is \(N = {@N_val@}\), meaning the student used \(N\) instead of \(N^2\) in the formula.

truefeedback.text: Correct! Since \(W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2\), doubling \(I\) gives \(W' = \tfrac{1}{2} L (2I)^2 = 4W\). The energy quadruples.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The energy stored in an inductor is \(W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2\). Since \(L\) does not depend on \(I\), doubling the current gives \(W' = \tfrac{1}{2} L (2I)^2 = 4 \times \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2 = 4W\). The energy quadruples.

hint.text: Hint 1 (Intuition): The self-inductance of a solenoid depends on its geometry (length, cross-sectional area) and the number of turns.
Hint 2 (Formulas): Use \(L = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell\) where \(A = \pi r^2\). For energy, recall \(W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2\). Be careful with unit conversions (cm → m).
Hint 3 (Worked Step): First convert \(r\) and \(\ell\) to metres. Then \(A = \pi r^2\) and \(L = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times N^2 \times A / \ell\). Finally \(W = \tfrac{1}{2} \times L \times I^2\).
